plan: Reduce SPY to 20% to meet concentration limits.
Increase QQQI and QQQ to 20% each for broad index and income.
Trim SMH to 10% to reduce semiconductor exposure.
Sell SOXL and SPAL to eliminate all leveraged ETF exposure.
Add CRM at 5% for high-quality growth, within limits.
conservative.read: This conservative plan targets around 1.5% per month, prioritizing capital preservation. Expected drawdowns are significantly reduced due to lower leverage and concentration. However, growth potential is limited.

plan: Trim SPY to 25% to meet concentration limits.
Increase QQQI to 25% for balanced income and index exposure.
Adjust QQQ to 15% for overall portfolio balance.
Reduce SMH to 10% to lower semiconductor concentration.
Sell SOXL and SPAL to remove high-risk leveraged ETFs.
Buy META at 10% based on strong scanner signal.
balanced.read: This balanced plan aims for approximately 2.5% per month, focusing on risk-first growth. Leverage is reduced, and concentration is managed, offering a moderate risk-reward profile. Swings will be less volatile than current, but still present.

moves.note: Reduce broad index for growth focus

plan: Trim SPY to 25% to meet concentration limits.
Reduce QQQI and QQQ to 10% each to free up capital for growth.
Increase SMH to 15% and add SOXL to 8% for aggressive semiconductor exposure.
Sell SPAL due to underperformance and to consolidate leverage.
Buy CRM at 12% as a top-scoring scanner setup.
aggressive.read: This aggressive plan targets around 4% per month, leaning into high-conviction setups and leverage. Expect significantly higher volatility and potential drawdowns, amplified by leveraged ETFs' daily decay. Strict stop discipline is crucial to manage outsized risk.

plan: Trim SPY to 25% to meet concentration limits.
Increase QQQI to 30% for primary income generation.
Reduce QQQ to 10% to shift focus to income.
Sell SMH and CRWD to eliminate high-beta growth exposure.
Sell SOXL and SPAL to remove all leveraged ETFs.
income.read: This income plan targets approximately 1% per month, prioritizing steady distributions and low drawdown. Growth potential is minimal, and NAV can still decline in market downturns. This is for yield, not capital appreciation.
